coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of carbon nanodots from cotton for multicolor imaging, patterning, and sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wen, Xiangping
Shi, Lihong
Wen, Guangming
Li, Yanyan
Dong, Chuan
Yang, Jun
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: An extreme green and economical strategy for producing fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs) by pyrolysis and microwave treatment of cotton is proposed for the first time. The fabricated CDs exhibit exceptional advantages including high fluorescent quantum yield, excellent biocompatibility, low-toxicity, and satisfactory chemical stability. Depending on these superior properties, the CDs have been applied in multicolor imaging, patterning, and sensing, respectively. According to the confocal fluorescent imaging of human uterine cervical squamous cell carcinoma cells, the CDs can penetrate into the cell and enter the cytoplasm and nucleus, suggesting pleasurable promise for bioimaging. Additionally, the obtained CDs have been used as fluorescent inks for drawing luminescent patterns, showing favorable application in anti-counterfeit and optoelectronic dimensions. Most strikingly, the as-prepared CDs can sensitively and selectively detect Fe3+ ions in a linear range of 5.0×10−8–1.0×10−5 M with a detection limit as low as 2.7×10−8 M.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Fluorescent
Multicolor imaging
Patterning
Sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and PL enhancement of CDs
The synthesis and PL enhancement of CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram for the synthesis of CDs from pyrolysis of cotton and the PL enhancement by microwave irradiation, along with photographs of the corresponding samples under UV light.
(a) TEM image of CDs. (b) The corresponding size distribution histogram of CDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of CDs. (b) C1s and (c) O1s spectra of CDs. (d) FTIR spectrum of CDs.
(a) Absorbance, excitation and emission spectra of the CDs. (b) Excitation dependent emission spectra of the CDs.

Laser scanning confocal microscopy image of A193 cells labeled with the CDs (0.35mgmL−1) under 405nm excitation (a) and 488nm excitation (b). (c) The merged image of a and b. (d) bright field. (e) The merged image of a and d. (f) The merged image of b and d. (g) The merged image of e and f.
Different photographs of the fluorescent patterns under UV light excitation (a–c) and 470nm excitation (d–f).

(a) Selectivity of CDs solution (0.05mg/mL) for Fe3+ over other ionic species under pH 5.0 condition at 330nm excitation. ([Mn+]=3.0×10−4 M). (b) Representative fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in the presence of various concentrations of Fe3+ (5.0×10−8 M–4.0×10−4 M). (c) The relationship between F0-F and Fe3+ from 5.0×10−8 M to 1.0×10−5 M. Inset: plot of the fluorescent intensity decrease (F 0 − F) versus the logarithm of concentrations of Fe3+ added.
Recoveries of Fe3+ in human serum samples and urine samples detected by the proposed method (n =5).

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon nanodots from cotton for multicolor imaging, patterning, and sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An extreme green and economical strategy for producing fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs) by pyrolysis and microwave treatment of cotton is proposed for the first time. The fabricated CDs exhibit exceptional advantages including high fluorescent quantum yield, excellent biocompatibility, low-toxicity, and satisfactory chemical stability. Depending on these superior properties, the CDs have been applied in multicolor imaging, patterning, and sensing, respectively. According to the confocal fluorescent imaging of human uterine cervical squamous cell carcinoma cells, the CDs can penetrate into the cell and enter the cytoplasm and nucleus, suggesting pleasurable promise for bioimaging. Additionally, the obtained CDs have been used as fluorescent inks for drawing luminescent patterns, showing favorable application in anti-counterfeit and optoelectronic dimensions. Most strikingly, the as-prepared CDs can sensitively and selectively detect Fe3+ ions in a linear range of 5.0×10−8–1.0×10−5 M with a detection limit as low as 2.7×10−8 M.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials, mainly including nanodiamonds [1], carbon nanotubes [2], fullerene [3], and fluorescent graphene [4–7], have been playing critical roles in various fields. Fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs), emerging as a rising star in the carbon nanomaterial family, were first obtained during purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 [8]. CDs are generally defined as carbon nanomaterials with sizes below 10nm and considered to consist of an amorphous or crystalline core with predominant sp2 carbon and an oxidized carbon surface with carboxyl group [9]. CDs have recently generated increasing attention owing to their superiority in chemical inertness, low cost, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, ease of functionalization, and resistance to photo-bleaching [10]. These distinct benefits of CDs distinguish them from traditional fluorescent materials and make them great promise in numerous exciting applications [11], such as optoelectronic devices [12], medical diagnosis [13], bioimaging [14], sensing [15–17], and photocatalysis [18].

Recent advances in the synthesis of CDs allow them to be formed from fine carbon structures by top-down methods, or from chemical precursors by bottom-up approaches. Top-down approaches involve laser ablation or electrochemical oxidation of graphite [19,20], electrochemical treatment of multiwalled carbon nanotubes [21], and chemical oxidation of commercially activated carbon [22]. Bottom-up methods include pyrolysis [23,24], wet oxidation [25,26], hydrothermal synthesis [27–30], and microwave-assisted synthesis [31,32]. However, most of these conventional synthesis methods have raised concerns over environmental harm, high cost, and tedious methodology. The preparation of CDs is overwhelmingly preferred to be environmentally benign and economical as far as possible. To this end, a green method using precursors directly from nature is a very promising solution and would be of great benefit to large scale synthesis and widespread applications.

During the past few years, major attention has been focused on developing green methods to fabricate CDs using natural precursors [33–37]. Sun et al. [38] developed an economical, and green preparative strategy toward photoluminescent (PL) CDs with a quantum yield (QY) of approximately 6.9% by hydrothermal process using low cost wastes of pomelo peel as a carbon source. Zhou et al. [39] reported a completely green synthetic method for producing fluorescent nitrogen-doped CDs by hydrothermal treatment of milk with a QY of approximately 12%. Shuang et al. [40] proposed a green and facile strategy for fabrication of dual-emission CDs with a QY of 3.0% by pyrolysis and microwave process using naked oats as the precursor. Sun et al. [41] demonstrated the use of flour as carbon source for microwave-assisted rapid green synthesis of CDs with a QY of approximately 5.4%. Yang et al. [34] described a facile approach for the synthesis of fluorescent CDs using watermelon peel as carbon source with a QY of 7.1%. However, QY of CDs prepared by these green methods was often relatively low. Considering that QY is of great importance for fluorescence bioimaging and biosensing, a green method for fabricating CDs with distinguished satisfactory QY is a challenging but worthy issue.

In this contribution, we have developed an extreme green and economical strategy for the fabrication of high luminescent CDs by pyrolysis and microwave treatment of cotton. The as-synthesized CDs show high fluorescent QY, excellent biocompatibility, low-toxicity, and satisfactory stability. Due to their favorable properties, the CDs have been applied in versatile fields, such as multicolor imaging, patterning, and sensing.

AlCl3, BaCl2, CaCl2, CdCl2, CoCl2, CuCl2, FeCl3, HgCl2, KCl, MgCl2, MnCl2, NaCl, NiCl2, and ZnCl2 were purchased from Beijing Chemical Corp. (Beijing, China). NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4 were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cotton was purchased from local market (Taiyuan, China). 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was obtained from Solarbio (Beijing, China). Distilled deionized (DDI) water was obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q-RO4 water purification system with a resistivity higher than 18MΩcm−1 (Bedford, MA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and PL enhancement of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized by pyrolysis of cotton. In a typical run, a crucible loaded with certain amounts of cotton was transferred into a muffle furnace, and was pyrolyzed at 300°C for 2h. After cooling down to room temperature, the black products were mechanically ground to fine powders. 0.5g of the resultant sample was dispersed in 5mL DDI water and magnetically stirred to form a black solution. The CDs were collected by removing larger particles through centrifugation at 4000rpm for 10min. Subsequently, the CDs aqueous solution was subjected to dialysis for 2 days.
PL enhancement of CDs was achieved by further microwave treatment. Typically, the obtained CDs aqueous solution was treated in a domestic microwave oven (700W) for 12min.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) study was carried out in a JEOL JEM-2100 instrument operating at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. Samples for TEM measurements were prepared by placing a drop of colloidal solution on carbon-coated copper grid and then dried at room temperature. The size distribution of CDs was performed by counting over 100 particles. Fluorescent photographs of CDs under UV light of 365nm were operated with ZF-2 ultraviolet analyzer for three purposes from Shanghai City Anting electronic instrument factory. Fourier Transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on Nicolet Magna 550 spectrometer using a resolution of 4cm−1. The sample with 1mg diluted by KBr (ratio 1:200) was pressed into the disk. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained with an AXIS ULTRA DLD electron spectrometer from Shimadzu Company using 300W Al Kα radiation. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Puxi TU-1901 UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer (China). Steady-state fluorescence measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-4500 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed on an Edinburgh FLS920 spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). A nanosecond hydrogen flash lamp was used as the excitation light source in the determination of fluorescence lifetime.

The relative fluorescence QY (Φ) of the CDs was calculated using the equation of Φ
x
= Φ std
I
x
A std
η 2
x /(I std
A
x
η 2 std). The optical densities were measured on Puxi TU-1901 UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer. In the equation, I
x and I std are the fluorescence intensities of the CDs and the standard, respectively. A
x and A std denote the optical densities (OD) of the CDs and the standard, respectively. Quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 was chosen as a standard with a QY of 0.54at 360nm. η
x and η std denote the refractive index of the CDs and the standard, respectively. The absorbencies of all the samples in 1.0cm cuvette were kept under 0.1at the excitation wavelength to minimize re-absorption effects.

For the cell cytotoxicity text, A193 cells were first plated on a Costar 96-well tissue-culture cluster and cultured at 37°C with 5% CO2 in air for 3h to adhere cells onto the surface. The well without cells and treatment with CDs was taken as a zero set. The medium was then changed with 100μL of fresh DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS containing CDs, and the cells were allowed to grow for another 24h. At least five parallel samples were performed in each group. Cells without treatment with CDs were taken as a control. After adding 20μL of 5.0mg/mL MTT reagent into every well, the cells were further incubated for 4h, followed by removing the culture medium with MTT, and then 150μL of DMSO was added. The resulting mixture was shaken for ca. 10min at room temperature. The OD of the mixture was measured at 490nm with a SunRisemicroplate reader (Tecan Austria GmbH, Grödig, Austria). The cell viability was estimated using the equation of Cell Viability (%)=(OD Treated/OD Control)×100%, where OD Control and OD Treated were obtained in the absence and presence of CDs, respectively.

A193 cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS and incubated at 37°C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. The CDs aqueous solution (500μL, 1.4mg/mL) was added to the culture medium (2.0mL) at 0.35mg/mL final concentration. After incubation for 0.5h, the A193 cells were harvested using 0.25% trypsin/0.020% EDTA, washed three times (1.0mL each) with pH 7.4 PBS (comprising 137mM NaCl, 2.7mM KCl, 8.0mM Na2HPO4, and 2.0mM KH2PO4), and kept in PBS for optical imaging by a Olympus FV1000 confocal microscope (Tokyo, Japan) with 40× objective.

We applied these CDs as fluorescent carbon inks for drawing multicolor fluorescent patterns. Commercially available papers (the paper showed no background UV fluorescence) were chosen as the patterning paper. The papers were coated with an aqueous solution of CDs and then dried in air. CDs solution (1.6mgmL−1) was filled into the pen for drawing versatile fluorescence patterns at room temperature. Photographs were taken using a UV light and multispectral fluorescence vivo molecular imaging system (S-0010A). Blue fluorescence patterns were obtained using UV light excitation (excitation: 365nm; emission: 460nm). Green fluorescence patterns were obtained using appropriate filters for CD (excitation: 470nm; emission: 510nm long-pass filter; acquisition settings: 500–750nm in 10nm steps). Exposure times were automatically calculated and the acquisition setting was 1500ms.

The detection of Fe3+ was performed at room temperature in 10mM phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (containing 150mM NaCl, pH 5.0). In a typical run, 10μL of CDs dispersion (1.4mg/mL) was added into 300μL of PBS, followed by the addition of Fe3+ standard with various concentrations. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded after reaction for 1min at room temperature. The sensitivity and selectivity measurements were conducted in triplicate.

Fresh human blood samples were obtained from the local hospital. In brief, two equivalent volume of water was mixed with fresh blood, then added the trichloroacetic acid solution (20%, isopyknic to the water before) drop-wise with shaking. And the mixture was left in an oscillator to shake 45min continuously. In order to separate the proteins from the serum, gentrifications at 3000rpm for 15min was carried out and the supernatant was collected. On account of the iron existing as ferrous in serum, 5μL 3% H2O2 solution was added to 1.0mL supernatant to oxidize the Fe2+ to Fe3+ for further analysis. Finally, the prepared samples were used for detections of Fe3+ according to the general procedure without additional special treatment.

The urine samples were collected from healthy volunteers. The samples were diluted 1000-fold with water before analysis. No sample pretreatments such as deproteinization or centrifugation were required.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis and PL enhancement of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 , the successful preparation of highly fiuorescent CDs was carried out by using cotton as the starting materials via pyrolysis and microwave treatment. Cotton mainly consists of fiber, which may serve as widespread, easily available, low-cost, and green natural carbon sources for large-scale fabrication of CDs. After pyrolysis of cotton, obtained CDs show weak blue luminance. The PL enhancement is observed by further microwave treatment of obtained CDs. The QY of CDs varies with their size and the surface chemistry involved. Uniform particle size and the increasing surface functional groups can improve the QY of CDs. The microwave treatment provides intensive, homogeneous and efficient energy, and thus it can achieve the elevated temperature and initiate the reaction in an extremely short time. The rapid treatment using microwave irradiation could contribute to uniform size of CDs and further increase of the functional groups on the surface, resulting in the better PL performance [28]. The time of microwave irradiation may manipulate the coverage of functional groups on CDs surface. With the increase of microwave irradiation time, QY of CDs increases. After 12min, QY of CDs leveled off which means that functional groups of CDs surface tend to be saturate. In comparison with the CDs without microwave irradiation treatment, the QY of the as-synthesized CDs has increased substantially. The resultant CDs show bright blue luminescence, indicating that the CDs possess outstanding optical properties. The as-prepared CDs freely disperse in water with transparent appearance. The pH value of the fresh CDs is ca. 4.6 resulting from the formed carboxyl groups on the surface area.

The size distribution and morphology of the obtained CDs were characterized by TEM (Fig. 2 ). As shown in Fig. 2a, CDs are spherical and well dispersed. Fig. 2b demonstrates that the size distribution of the CDs is relatively small and the majority falls within the range from 3.5 to 6.5nm. Based on the statistical analysis of above one hundred particles, the average diameter of the CDs is 4.9±0.10nm.a

The surface functional groups and element analysis for the obtained CDs were characterized by XPS. The XPS spectrum shows two peaks at 288.82 and 533.73eV, which are attributed to C1s and O1s (Fig. 3a). The XPS analysis of the elemental composition of CDs demonstrates that these CDs are mainly composed of C and O element (the atom ratio of C:O is 2.02:1). The deconvolution of the C1s spectrum of the CDs indicates the presence of three types of carbon bonds: CC (284.7eV), CC (286.0eV), and OCO (288.8, 292.7, and 295.5eV) (Fig. 3b). In the O1s spectrum (Fig. 3c), two peaks at 531.6 and 533.0eV are ascribed to CO and COH/COC, respectively. The XPS results confirmed that the surface of the CDs is mainly covered with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. The FTIR spectrum was used to identify the functional groups of CDs (Fig. 3d). The peaks at 3430 and 1712cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and CO, the peaks at about 2975 and 1582cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of CH and CC, the peaks at 1146 and 1268cm−1 indicated the existence of CO, and the peak at 1405cm−1 is ascribed to the bending vibrations of CH2 group. The functional groups identified by the FTIR are in good agreement with XPS. The zeta potential of CDs is −33.6mV, which can be attributed to the existence of OH/COOH groups on the surface of CDs. Obviously, the surface hydrophilic groups could stabilize the CDs in aqueous solution.

The optical properties of CDs are displayed in Fig. 4 . As shown in Fig. 4a, CDs exhibit a strong absorption peak at 252nm together with a shoulder around 283nm. The peak at 252nm is ascribed to π–π* transition of the CC bands of localized sp2 clusters in the core, whereas the shoulder is due to n–π* transition of the CO bands of carboxyl groups on the shell of CDs. A strong emission spectrum centered at 460nm is observed when excited at 330nm (Fig. 4a). Additionally, CDs exhibit excitation-dependent emission (Fig. 4b). When the excitation wavelength is changed from 330nm to 488nm, the maximum emission peak position shifts to longer wavelength, from 460nm to 528nm, and the fluorescence intensity decreases remarkably. Using quinine sulfate as the reference, the fluorescence QY of the CDs in aqueous solution is measured to be 14.8%, which is higher than other CDs derived from green method [34,38–41].

The fluorescence lifetime (τ) of CDs was assessed by fluorescent decay curves (Fig. S1a). The decay curve is fitted using biexponential functions Y(t) based on non-linear least squares analysis in equation: Y(t)= A 1exp(−t/τ 1)+ A 2exp(−t/τ 2), where A 1 and A 2 are the fractional contributions of time-resolved decay life-time of τ 1 and τ 2. Further, according to equation: τ average =(A 1 τ 1 2 + A 2 τ 2 2)/(A 1 τ 1 + A 2 τ 2), we calculate the average lifetime (τ average) of CDs as 6.14±0.05ns (χ 2 <1.08), which is comparable to reported values [42]. The stability of the prepared CDs under different conditions was investigated. The PL intensity of CDs under pH values from 3.0 to 12.0 is measured (Fig. S1b). Interestingly, it is seen that the PL intensity of CDs keeps constant in the pH range from 6.0 to 11.0. Additionally, PL intensity of CDs remains almost constant even after UV excitation for four hours (Fig. S1c) and storage for three months (Fig. S1d). The results indicate that the CDs have excellent stability, making them promising for meaningful applications and commercial purpose.

Cytotoxicity is an indispensible parameter for future application of CDs especially in live cell-labeling. In vitro toxicity experiments of the CDs were evaluated using human cervical cancer (A193) cell lines through the MTT assay. There exists a dose-dependent cytotoxicity on the A193 cells after the initial 24-h exposure to the CDs. As shown in Fig. S2, the cell viability decreases from 100% to 83% with concentrations of CDs from 0.1 to 0.8mg/mL. The results demonstrated the low toxicity of CDs to cultured cells under the experimental conditions at the concentration of 0.35mg/mL.

To demonstrate the potential application of CDs as a bioimaging probe, in vitro cellular uptake experiments in A193 cells were performed. A193 cells were labeled with the prepared CDs and the images were taken under a laser scanning confocal microscope. As shown in Fig. 5 , after being labeled with CDs, no cell damage was observed. Clearly, it is seen that cells show bright blue PL when excited with a 405nm laser (Fig. 5a) and bright green PL when excited with a 488nm laser (Fig. 5b). The merged images with the bright field and the fluorescence images (Fig. 5e and f), display that the CDs can label both the cytoplasmic and the nucleus of A193 cells. Thus as-prepared CDs can be used as excellent fluorescence probes for bioimaging.

So far, single-walled carbon nanotubes, luminescent CdTe nanocrystals/polymer composites, and fluorescein have been patterned [43]. Whereas, to our knowledge, the use of CDs for multicolor patterning is still relatively rare. We applied the prepared CDs as fluorescent carbon inks for multicolor hand-drawn patterns. The commercially available papers (no background UV fluorescence) were coated with an aqueous solution of CDs and then dried in air. As shown in Fig. 6 , bright blue fluorescent patterns are observed under UV light excitation (Fig. 6a–c) while bright green fluorescent patterns are observed under 470nm excitation (Fig. 6d–f). After drawing fluorescent patterns for three mouths, bright blue or green fluorescent patterns are observed which is almost the same. This finding indicates that the CDs-based fluorescent ink can be used in anti-counterfeit fields as well as optoelectronic devices.

To evaluate the selectivity of the sensing system, the fluorescence spectra in the presence of representative metal ions were examined under pH 5.0 condition at 330nm excitation, including Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, K+ and Na+, as shown in Fig. 7a. It can be seen that a much lower fluorescence is observed for CDs upon addition of Fe3+ ions while other metal ions show either no or slight change in the PL intensity relative to the free CDs. This result reveals that the as-synthesized CDs are highly selective toward Fe3+ ions over the other metal ions and could be developed as an efficient fluorescence sensor for Fe3+ ions. Fe3+ ions quench the fluorescence of CDs through electron or energy transfer. Previous reports show that hydroxyl exhibits good binding affinity for Fe3+ [44]. Although the surface of the CDs is mainly covered with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups from the FTIR and XPS in our experiment. Actually, hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs can selectively interact with Fe3+ while not carboxyl groups. The Fe3+ ions sensing capacity of the as-prepared CDs was investigated in the range of 5.0×10−8 M to 4.0×10−4 M under pH 5.0 condition at 330nm excitation. With increasing Fe3+ ions concentration, as shown in Fig. 7b, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases progressively, revealing that the sensing system is sensitive to Fe3+ concentration. Fig. 7c presented the PL data versus the concentration of Fe3+ ions. The correlation coefficient (R 2) is 0.995 over the concentration range of 5.0×10−8 M–1.0×10−5 M, while the plot does not fit a linear equation over the whole concentration range of 5.0×10−8 M–4.0×10−4 M, indicating that both dynamic and static quenching processes occur in this sensor system [36]. The detection limit of Fe3+ was 2.7×10−8 M at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The results show that the sensor is highly sensitive and has widely linear range.

For testing the practicality of this developed approach, standard recovery experiments were performed in human blood samples and urine samples (Table 1 ). As listed, the recoveries of human blood samples and urine samples were 98.36–107.33% and 94.00–104.00%, respectively, and there were little inference from other substrates of the blood, indicating the proposed method may broaden ways for practical detections of Fe3+ in real samples.

In summary, we have described a green and low cost synthesis route to fabricate highly fiuorescent CDs using cotton as the precursor and demonstrate their applications in multicolor imaging, patterning, and sensing, respectively. The CDs have outstanding stability such that their PL intensity does not change even after UV radiation for four hours or storage for three months. The as-prepared CDs possess good biocompatibility and can be used as excellent fluorescence probes for multicolor imaging. Moreover, these CDs have been used as fluorescent inks for drawing multicolor patterning. Meanwhile, the obtained CDs have been further utilized as a novel sensing probe for sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions with a detection limit as low as 2.7×10−8 M. Therefore, we demonstrate herein an extreme eco-friendly strategy to produce highly fiuorescent CDs from widely available green carbon sources for versatile applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21175086, 21475080 and 21306108), Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province of China (2013011009−5), Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (No.2013−key 1).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.07.019.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Diamond nanoparticles: jewels for chemistry and physics
A route to brightly fluorescent carbon nanotubes for near-infrared imaging in mice
Synthesis and characterization of a photoluminescent nanoparticle based on fullerene-silica hybridization
Graphene oxide as a chemically tunable platform for optical applications
Surface chemistry routes to modulate the photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots: from fluorescence mechanism to up-conversion bioimaging applications
Upconversion and downconversion fluorescent graphene quantum dots: ultrasonic preparation and photocatalysis
Synthesis of large, stable colloidal graphene quantum dots with tunable size
Preparation of carbon nanodots from single chain polymeric nanoparticles and theoretical investigation of the photoluminescence mechanism
Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
Photoluminescence properties of graphene versus other carbon nanomaterials
Deep ultraviolet photoluminescence of water-soluble self-passivated graphene quantum dots
Carbon dot-based inorganic–organic nanosystem for two-photon imaging and biosensing of pH variation in living cells and tissues
Photoluminescent carbon dots directly derived from polyethylene glycol and their application for cellular imaging
A simple and sensitive fluorescent sensor for methyl parathion based on l-tyrosine methyl ester functionalized carbon dots
Synthesis-modification integration: one-step fabrication of boronic acid functionalized carbon dots for fluorescent blood sugar sensing
Carbon dots based dual-emission silica nanoparticles as a ratiometric nanosensor for Cu2+
Exploring the conformation-regulated function of titin kinase by mechanical pump and probe experiments with single molecules
One-step synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by laser irradiation
Facile preparation of low cytotoxicity fluorescent carbon nanocrystals by electrooxidation of graphite
An electrochemical avenue to blue luminescent nanocrystals from multiwalled carbon nanotubes
Commercially activated carbon as the source for producing multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots by chemical oxidation
Plant leaf-derived fluorescent carbon nanodots for sensing, patterning and coding
Facile synthesis of highly emissive carbon dots from pyrolysis of glycerol; gram scale production of carbon dots/mSiO2 for cell imaging and drug release
Hair fiber as a precursor for synthesizing of sulfur-and nitrogen-co-doped carbon nanodots with tunable luminescence properties
In vivo imaging of tumour bearing near-infrared fluorescence-emitting carbon nanodots derived from tire soot
One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of highly luminescent nitrogen-doped amphoteric carbon nanodots for bioimaging from Bombyx mori silk-natural proteins
Bifunctional fluorescent carbon nanodots: green synthesis via soy milk and application as metal-free electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction
One-step synthesis of amino-functionalized fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by hydrothermal carbonization of chitosan
Intrinsically fluorescent carbon dots with tunable emission derived from hydrothermal treatment of glucose in the presence of monopotassium phosphate
A biocompatible fluorescent ink based on water-soluble luminescent carbon nanodots
Amino acids as the source for producing carbon nanodots: microwave assisted one-step synthesis, intrinsic photoluminescence property and intense chemiluminescence enhancement
An absolutely green approach to fabricate carbon nanodots from soya bean grounds
Facile synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots using watermelon peel as a carbon source

Hydrothermal treatment of grass: a low-cost, green route to nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, photoluminescent polymer nanodots as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for label-free detection of Cu(II) ions
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents
Easy synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from gelatin and their luminescent properties and applications
Economical, green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions
Green synthesis of luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots from milk and its imaging application
Naked oats-derived dual-emission carbon nanodots for ratiometric sensing and cellular imaging
Microwave-assisted rapid green synthesis of photoluminescent carbon nanodots From flour and their applications for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions
One step synthesis of C-dots by microwave mediated caramelization of poly(ethylene glycol)
Inkjet printing of electrically conductive patterns of carbon nanotubes
Inkjet printing of luminescent CdTe nanocrystal-polymer composites
A release-induced response for the rapid recognition of latent fingerprints and formation of inkjet-printed patterns
Iron(III) selective molecular and supramolecular fluorescent probes

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiangping Wen obtained B.Sc. degree in Chemistry from Changzhi University in 2013. Now she is a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in photochemistry.

Lihong Shi is now working as an associate professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. She obtained her Ph.D. degree in Physical Chemistry from Shanxi Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2008. Her current research interests include nanomaterial and chemosensors.

Guangming Wen is now working as an associate professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. He obtained his Ph.D. degree at Shanxi University in 2008. His current research interests include Biosensor and electroanalytical chemistry.
Yanyan Li completed B.Sc. degree in Chemistry from Jinzhong University in 2013. Now she is a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in analytical chemistry.

Chuan Dong is a professor of Analytical Chemistry at Shanxi University. He obtained his Ph.D. degree at Shanxi University in 2002. Dong has published over 200 papers and 100 patents and his current research interests are biosensors, chemosensors, optical fiber sensor, fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence, and environmental analysis and monitoring.

Jun Yang is an associate professor of mechanical and materials engineering at University of Western Ontario. He is the entrants of “Hundred talents program” in Shanxi Province. He has conducted Multiple academic communication in Institute of Environmental Science of Shanxi University.

Shaomin Shuang is a professor of Analytical Chemistry at Shanxi University. She received her Ph.D. degree in South China University of Technology in 1998. She has published extensively in journals such as Nanoscale, Journal of Materials Chemistry B, Chemical Communications, Sensors and Actuators B, Analytical Chemistry, Talanta etc. Her research interests are in the areas of analytical chemistry, supermolecular chemistry, and photochemistry.
